T1.subclaims: (a) Category rows with zero aggregate but nonzero signed leaf-function rows are erased in both engines — CONFIRMED. (b) This violates the spec's omission rule — CONFIRMED: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:574-579 says 'A row may be omitted only when its aggregate AND its entire rendered subtree are zero: signed children can cancel to a zero parent total (a +10/-10 bucket, a zero-sum domain), and such a parent renders with its nonzero rows rather than erasing them.' The R6 fix moved bucket filtering to 'keep while any category row rendered' (render.py comment, scopes.rs:699-708) and fixed the domain filter (cpu_time != 0 OR any cost-kind nonzero), but the category-level filter was left as a bare aggregate check, so the fixed bucket keep-rule sees zero category rows and drops the bucket too. (c) Additivity is not broken (empty buckets sum trivially), but +10/-10 of real signed data disappears from the report entirely — exactly the erasure the spec forbids.
T1.evidence: Config .goalloop-support/triage8/T1/scopes.yml: cost_kind {Work: [name:Pos, name:Neg]}, scope S with rollup {All: [Work]}, against .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/profile.pb (samples Pos+10 and Neg-10 under S). `uv run clankerprof scopes --profile .goalloop-support/triage6/Q3/profile.pb --config .goalloop-support/triage8/T1/scopes.yml` -> exit 0, boundary S = {"total": 0, "samples": 2, "buckets": []}. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- scopes ...same args...` -> exit 0, identical {"total": 0, "samples": 2, "buckets": []}. Root cause: clankerprof/render.py `_render_boundary_bucket` filters `stats.cpu_time != 0` per category (and the leftover/'Other' selection at render.py:~184 uses the same bare check); crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs `render_bucket` (~745) filters `.filter(|stats| stats.cpu_time != 0)`. The Work category's CategoryStats holds leaf functions Pos:+10/Neg:-10 (nonzero rendered subtree) yet is dropped, so the All bucket renders no categories and the bucket keep-rule then drops it. Spec citation: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:574-579 (quoted above).
T1.fix_sketch: Introduce one shared 'category is renderable' predicate — keep a category when stats.cpu_time != 0 OR any leaf-function metric cpu_time != 0 (its entire rendered subtree) — and use it in three places, identically in both languages: Python render.py `_render_boundary_bucket` category_rows filter and the leftover/'Other' selection in `_render_boundary`; Rust scopes.rs `render_bucket` filter_map filter and the corresponding leftover selection. Bucket/domain keep-rules already handle zero parents with rendered children (R6 fix), so a zero-aggregate category then renders inside its bucket with the +10/-10 leaf_functions, matching the spec's subtree rule. Byte parity: same ordering (categories keep configured rollup order; leftover keeps cpu_time-desc order which is unaffected for equal zero keys only if the sort is stable in both — Python sorted and Rust sort_by are both stable). Add a parity test with a single zero-sum cost kind and pin bytes; update docs/CLANKERPROF_PARITY.md.

T2.subclaims: (a) slices projection resolves overlapping definitions first-match in declaration order — CONFIRMED (slices.py `_resolve_frame_slice` iterates options.slices in order; repro assigned all 7 ns to A). (b) Scope owner predicate slice:B matches on B's raw rules, ignoring that an earlier definition owns the frame — CONFIRMED in both engines (clankerprof/scopes.py:296-301 `any(definition.name == value and definition.matches_frame(...))`; crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:309-311 identical `.any(...)`). (c) Contract violation — CONFIRMED: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:403 defines Slice in scopes as 'Optional ownership source that owner predicates can reference via slice:<name>', and docs/CLANKERPROF.md:273-276 says 'If authoritative ownership matters, load a slice file ... and use slice:<name> predicates in [owner]' — attributing 100% to an owner whose slice owns zero frames is not authoritative ownership. No spec text or goalloop.audit.md refutation documents raw-rule matching as deliberate (checked; the only pinned slice: asymmetry is the collapse/descendant-rescue rule at SPEC.md:621-624, which concerns the slices projection). Parity holds (byte-identical outputs), so this is identical-but-harmful plus a documented-semantics violation. (d) Achievability: full sample-level 'effective attribution' does not map onto a per-frame owner predicate, but frame-level first-match (the exact rule slices.py uses per frame in `slice_without_descendant_attributes`) does, identically in both engines.
T2.evidence: Slices A and B with identical paths [/srv/app/**] (.goalloop-support/triage8/T2/slices.yml). `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --slices .goalloop-support/triage8/T2/slices.yml` -> exit 0, [('A', 7)] — B owns nothing. Scope config (.goalloop-support/triage8/T2/scopes.yml): slices: slices.yml; owner {BOwner: "slice:B"}; scope function T. `uv run clankerprof scopes --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --config .goalloop-support/triage8/T2/scopes.yml` -> exit 0, boundary T domains [('BOwner', 7, 100.0)]. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- scopes ...same args...` -> exit 0, byte-identical (cmp passed). Spec citations: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:403 ('ownership source'); docs/CLANKERPROF.md:273-276 ('authoritative ownership'); code: clankerprof/scopes.py:296-301, crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs:309-311, clankerprof/slices.py:214-226 (first-match resolution the predicate ignores).
T2.fix_sketch: Make the scope slice:<name> predicate test frame-level effective ownership: resolve the frame once through declaration-order first-match over the loaded slice definitions (skipping default-flagged definitions, falling back to the default slice name for unmatched frames, mirroring slices.py `_resolve_frame_slice` minus attribute rules, which scopes does not load) and match iff the resolved name equals the predicate value; cache per unique frame identity like the other predicates. Python: replace the any(...) arm in `_FramePredicateMatcher._matches_uncached` (scopes.py:296) with a memoized resolve-then-compare helper. Rust: mirror in scopes.rs:309 with the same iteration order and default fallback so bytes stay pinned. Spec: extend SPEC.md:403 (and CLANKERPROF.md:273) to pin 'slice:<name> matches a frame only when <name> is the frame's first-match owner under the loaded slice config (default slice included)'. Add a parity test with two overlapping slices where slice:B matches nothing and slice:A plus slice:(default) partition the frames.

T3.subclaims: (a) Python defaults a missing top-level `slices` key to [] — CONFIRMED: clankerprof/cli.py:508 `raw_slices = raw_payload.get("slices", [])` errors only on a present non-list value. (b) Rust rejects the same file — CONFIRMED: crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:119-125 `value.get("slices").and_then(as_sequence)` -> Err("Slices file must contain a slices array.") when the key is absent. (c) Contract violation — CONFIRMED twice over: it is a straight validation-parity break (same input, exit 0 vs exit 2), and it violates the spec's typo principle pinned at docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:421-424 ('a typo such as exclude_descendents must never silently disable its rule') — a `slice:` typo silently disables the entire supplied ownership configuration and attributes everything to the implicit (all) slice.
T3.evidence: File .goalloop-support/triage8/T3/typo.yml: 'slice:' (singular) with one app slice. Python: `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --slices .goalloop-support/triage8/T3/typo.yml` -> exit 0, slices [('(all)', 7)] — config silently ignored. Rust: `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices ...same args...` -> exit 2, {"error": "Slices file must contain a slices array.", "ok": false}. Code: clankerprof/cli.py:508-510 vs crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:119-125. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:421-424 (typo-never-silently-disables principle), and the shared error message already exists in both engines.
T3.fix_sketch: Python-only fix in `_load_slices` (clankerprof/cli.py:508): treat an absent key as the same error Rust already emits — `if "slices" not in raw_payload: raise ValueError("Slices file must contain a slices array.")` (keep the existing non-list check for present wrong-typed values; note Rust also errors on `slices: null` via as_sequence, and Python's isinstance check then matches once the get() default is removed). No Rust change; byte parity of the envelope is already exact. Add a parity test feeding a slices file without the key (and with `slices: null`) expecting exit 2 with the shared message in both engines.

T4.subclaims: (a) Both importers treat each missing (or null) member inside a present summary object as check-skipped — CONFIRMED: clankerprof/facts.py:506-514 `summary_payload.get(key)` returns None -> check skipped; crates/clankerprof-core/src/facts.rs:439-449 `None | Some(Value::Null) => Ok(None)` likewise. (b) `summary: {}` is accepted exactly like an absent summary — CONFIRMED by reproduction in both engines. (c) Contract violation — CONFIRMED: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:100-106 says 'The summary block is a redundancy check: exporters always write it, and importers cross-validate a present summary against the decoded samples (mismatches are validation errors). An absent (or explicit null) summary skips the cross-check identically ... A present summary of any other type is a validation error' — the spec's only sanctioned skip is summary-level absence/null; a present-but-member-truncated summary silently evades the documented cross-check, the same fail-open shape confirmed in R1-01 (missing slices/boundaries keys accepted as empty). The exporter (facts.py:215) always writes all four members, so no legitimate producer emits a partial summary. Parity holds, but identical-but-harmful counts: an attacker or truncation bug can strip the redundancy check without tripping validation.
T4.evidence: Built .goalloop-support/triage8/T4/facts_empty_summary.json from .goalloop-support/triage4/K14/facts_valid.json (original summary {'empty_sample_count': 0, 'non_empty_sample_count': 1, 'sample_count': 1, 'total_primary_value': 7} replaced with {}). `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage8/T4/facts_empty_summary.json` -> exit 0, [('(all)', 7)]. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage8/T4/facts_empty_summary.json` -> exit 0, identical report. Code: clankerprof/facts.py:491-530 (`_validate_summary`, every `summary_int` returns None on missing/None and the comparison is gated `is not None`), crates/clankerprof-core/src/facts.rs:426-460 (`validate_summary`, `None | Some(Value::Null) => Ok(None)`). Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:92-106 (summary field table plus the redundancy-check paragraph quoted above).
T4.fix_sketch: When the summary is a present object, require all four members (sample_count, total_primary_value, empty_sample_count, non_empty_sample_count) as integers; a missing or null member fails closed with a shared message, e.g. 'Sample facts summary must contain sample_count, total_primary_value, empty_sample_count, and non_empty_sample_count.' (single message keeps byte parity simple). Python: in `_validate_summary`, replace the None-skips arm of `summary_int` with a raise; Rust: mirror in `validate_summary` (drop the `None | Some(Value::Null) => Ok(None)` arm). Applies to both v2 and legacy v1 imports (both call the same validator and both exporters always wrote all four members). Spec: extend SPEC.md:~101 with 'a present summary must contain all four members; a missing or null member is a validation error, never a skipped check.' Add parity tests for summary {} and summary {"sample_count": null} expecting exit 2 in both engines.

T5.subclaims: (a) The hoist appends the leading global --output AFTER the subcommand's own tokens, so with `--output A <cmd> ... --output B` the first-typed A lands last and wins — CONFIRMED in both engines (clankerprof/cli.py `_hoist_global_output` returns `tokens + moved`; crates/clankerprof-core/src/main.rs `hoist_global_output` does `tokens.extend(moved)`, its comment even admits 'the moved token lands last, so it wins over an earlier local --output'). (b) Contract violation — CONFIRMED by the conjunction of two spec clauses: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:752-753 'A global --output before the subcommand is equivalent to the subcommand's own --output', and SPEC.md:799-801 'Repeating any non-accumulating option — scalar or boolean, focus flags included — keeps the last occurrence in both implementations.' If the global spelling is equivalent to a local one, the user's repetition order must resolve last-wins, i.e. the later local B; instead the earlier A wins. Control run confirms two local occurrences ARE last-wins, so the mixed-position case is the lone order reversal. The Rust code comment documents the mechanism but no spec/docs text pins global-beats-local as deliberate. Parity holds (identical wrong selection), so this is identical-but-harmful against the documented last-occurrence rule.
T5.evidence: `uv run clankerprof --output .goalloop-support/triage8/T5 compare --before .goalloop-support/triage4/K13/slice_report.json --after .goalloop-support/triage4/K13/slice_report.json --output .goalloop-support/triage8/T5/local/out.json` -> exit 2, {"error": "[Errno 21] Is a directory: '.goalloop-support/triage8/T5'", "ok": false} (earlier global directory selected). Same via `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- --output ...` -> exit 2, {"error": "Is a directory (os error 21)", "ok": false}. Control: `uv run clankerprof compare ... --output .goalloop-support/triage8/T5 --output .goalloop-support/triage8/T5/local/out.json` (both local) -> exit 0, receipt output=.goalloop-support/triage8/T5/local/out.json — last occurrence wins as spec'd. Code: clankerprof/cli.py:1650-1673 (`_hoist_global_output`, `return tokens + moved`), crates/clankerprof-core/src/main.rs:367-394 (`tokens.extend(moved)` plus the admitting comment). Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:752-753 and 798-801.
T5.fix_sketch: Insert the hoisted tokens immediately AFTER the subcommand token instead of at the end, in both engines: in Python `_hoist_global_output`, the scan loop already stops at the first non-dash token (the subcommand) with `index` pointing at it — return `tokens[:index+1] + moved + tokens[index+1:]` (fall back to `tokens + moved` when no subcommand token exists, preserving current usage-error behavior); in Rust `hoist_global_output`, `tokens.splice(index+1..index+1, moved)` symmetrically (its scan starts at 1 for argv[0]). This preserves the hoist's original purpose (the subparser default no longer clobbers the global value) while restoring typed-order last-wins: `--output A cmd --output B` becomes `cmd --output A ... --output B` -> B wins; multiple leading globals already keep only the last moved pair. Fix the now-stale Rust comment, pin the resolved order in SPEC.md:752 ('...and mixed global/local repetitions keep the last occurrence in the user's typed order'), and add a parity test for the mixed-position case in both directions.

T6.evidence: Repro: printf '\x7b\x7c' > group_prefix.pb; cat .goalloop-support/triage/F4/base.pb >> group_prefix.pb (prefixes an empty unknown group, field 15 SGROUP+EGROUP). `uv run clankerprof facts --profile .goalloop-support/triage8/T6/group_prefix.pb` -> {"error": "Unsupported protobuf wire type: 3", "ok": false}, exit 2. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- facts --profile ...` -> identical envelope, exit 2. The unprefixed base.pb decodes fine (exit 0), so the group bytes alone trigger rejection. Code: clankerprof/proto.py:77-90 skip() handles wire types 0/1/2/5 only, raising 'Unsupported protobuf wire type' otherwise; crates/clankerprof-core/src/proto.rs:111-127 is the identical match. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:42-47 defines the exhaustive strict-decode error list (long varints, over-length fields, truncated fixed32/64 'even in skipped unknown fields', field number 0, bad UTF-8) — group wire types are not listed, and the phrase 'skipped unknown fields' commits the decoder to skipping unknown fields, which per the protobuf wire spec includes SGROUP/EGROUP (legal proto2 wire format, reintroduced as delimited fields in protobuf editions; Go's protobuf runtime used by pprof tooling skips unknown groups). Identical in both languages (no parity break) but rejects legal input — identical-but-harmful. Severity minor is fair: proto3 pprof encoders never emit groups, so only foreign/extension producers hit this.
T6.subclaims: (1) Both skip routines reject wire types 3/4 — TRUE (proto.py:77-90, proto.rs:111-127, reproduced with identical envelopes, exit 2 both). (2) Group wire types are legal protobuf an unknown-field skipper must handle — TRUE (proto2 wire format + editions delimited fields; reference runtimes skip them). (3) The spec classifies this input as valid — TRUE by omission: spec's strict-decode list (lines 42-47) enumerates the malformed cases and groups are absent, while unknown-field skipping is promised; nothing documents group rejection as deliberate.
T6.fix_sketch: Both languages, byte-identical errors. In skip(): wire type 3 -> loop: read_key(); if the key's wire type is 4, require its field number to equal the group's opening field number (mismatch = decode error, e.g. 'Mismatched protobuf group end.') and return; otherwise recursively skip that field (so truncated fixed/varint inside groups still error per spec line 44). A wire type 4 reaching skip() outside a group is a decode error (e.g. 'Unmatched protobuf group end.'). Cap group nesting (e.g. 128, matching the existing recursion-limit precedent) so hostile input cannot overflow the Rust stack — use an explicit depth counter. Python: clankerprof/proto.py skip(); Rust: proto.rs skip() (take &mut self, iterative with a depth counter or depth-limited recursion). Add spec text under the decoding contract documenting group skipping + the two new error strings, a PARITY.md row, and parity + native tests (empty group, nested group, truncated group, stray EGROUP).

T7.evidence: Repro config .goalloop-support/triage8/T7/scopes.yml: {slices: ../../triage5/P7/slices.yml (two slices both named App), cost_kind: {Work: 'name:Leaf'}, scope: [{function: T}]}. `uv run clankerprof scopes --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --config .goalloop-support/triage8/T7/scopes.yml` -> exit 0, full report with total_time_ns 7. Same command via clankerprof-rs -> exit 0, byte-identical output (cmp passed). Expected per spec: exit 2 with 'Slice config declares duplicate slice name: App. ...'. Code: the duplicate/multiple-default/reserved-name checks live only in analyze_slice_facts (clankerprof/slices.py:343-368; crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs:195-232) and in the slices-command path (_validate_slice_options, cli.py:331+). The scopes loading path — _load_boundary_options at clankerprof/cli.py:1036-1052 calling _load_slices, and the Rust scopes config loader — never routes through either, so invalid definitions enter the scope matcher. The Rust comment at slices.rs:199-201 ('so file, config, and programmatic paths all share it') is falsified by this path. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:635-646 states unconditionally 'At most one slice may set default: true; declaring several is a validation error' / 'Slice names must be unique: duplicates are a validation error in both languages' / reserved pseudo-slice names 'a validation error in both languages' — no scoping to the slices command; spec 424-427 shows scope configs' slices key is a first-class validated surface.
T7.subclaims: (1) Duplicate-name slice file loads without error through scope config — TRUE, reproduced in both languages, exit 0, byte-identical 7 ns report. (2) Multiple defaults and reserved names also bypass — TRUE by code inspection: all three checks sit in the same analyze_slice_facts prologue that the scopes path never calls (not separately reproduced; same single bypass). (3) Documented contract violated — TRUE: spec 635-641 phrases all three as unconditional validation errors 'in both languages'. Parity itself holds (identical outputs), but identical-but-harmful applies: which duplicate definition feeds scope slice: predicates is exactly the ambiguity the rule was added to close.
T7.fix_sketch: Extract the three checks (multiple defaults, duplicate names, reserved pseudo-names) into a shared validator with unchanged error strings: Python `def validate_slice_definitions(slices: Sequence[SliceDefinition]) -> None` in clankerprof/slices.py, called from analyze_slice_facts and from _load_boundary_options (cli.py:1052, right after _load_slices); Rust `pub fn validate_slice_definitions(slices: &[SliceDefinition]) -> Result<(), String>` in slices.rs, called from analyze_slice_facts and from the scopes config loader after loading the slice file (scopes.rs). Identical error ordering (defaults first, then per-slice duplicate/reserved in declaration order) preserves envelope byte parity. Also apply to the report-mode path if it loads scope configs independently. Add parity test: scope config referencing a duplicate-name / multi-default / reserved-name slice file exits 2 with matching envelopes; update PARITY.md and spec text noting validation applies on every slice-loading surface.

T8.evidence: Python semantics verified: python3 -c "re.compile(r'(?P<a>/srv)(?#()(?P<b>/app)\\2')" -> groups: 2, matches '/srv/app/app'. Repro: facts file with path /srv/app/app (sed over triage4/K8/facts.json), config {"T": {"Matched": "regex:(?P<a>/srv)(?#()(?P<b>/app)\\2"}}. Python: `uv run clankerprof targets --facts ... --config ... --format json` -> exit 0, Matched category with time_ns 7, pct 100.0. Rust: same command via clankerprof-rs -> exit 2, {"error": "Invalid regex pattern '(?P<a>/srv)(?#()(?P<b>/app)\\2': Error compiling regex: Numbered backref/call not allowed because named group was used, use a named backref instead", "ok": false}. Mechanism isolated: same pattern WITHOUT the comment compiles in Rust via the rewrite (exit 0, identical attribution), and the comment pattern with \\k<b> instead of \\2 also compiles in Rust (exit 0) — so fancy-regex handles (?#...) fine and the sole failure is rewrite_numeric_backrefs_to_named (targets.rs:~487-520) counting the '(' inside '(?#(' as capture group 2, shifting b to 3 so names.get(2) misses and \\2 survives unrewritten. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:516-520 guarantees 'numeric backreferences to named groups are rewritten to named backrefs so Python's mixed spelling compiles identically'; the documented engine-edge exceptions (spec 525-537: $ anchor, (?i) İ folding, \\w/\\s/\\b edges, conditional groups) do not include comments; PARITY.md:153 claims the rewrite covered. Genuine parity defect, achievable fix (no engine limitation).
T8.subclaims: (1) Python treats the pattern as 2 groups and matches — TRUE (re.compile groups=2, search matches, CLI exit 0 with 7 ns/100% Matched). (2) Rust exits 2 with the numbered-backref error — TRUE, exact envelope reproduced. (3) Root cause is the rewrite's paren counting inside (?#...), not a fancy-regex dialect limit — TRUE, proven by the two control runs (comment-free numeric backref rewrites fine; comment + named backref compiles fine). (4) Outside documented exceptions — TRUE, comments absent from the spec 525-537 edge list.
T8.fix_sketch: Rust-only (Python uses re directly). In rewrite_numeric_backrefs_to_named (crates/clankerprof-core/src/targets.rs), teach both scan passes to skip Python comment groups: when not in a character class and chars[i..] starts with '(?#', advance i past the next ')' (CPython sre_parse ends a comment at the first ')' with no escape processing; if none, the pattern is malformed — return None and let the original engine error stand). Apply in pass 1 (group counting — fixes this bug) and pass 2 (emission — prevents rewriting a literal \\N inside a comment). Keep the existing class/escape handling. Add native unit tests (comment before/among named groups, unterminated (?#, \\N inside a comment) plus a parity-suite case pinning Python/Rust identical attribution for the repro pattern; note comment support under the rewrite sentence in CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:516-520 and the PARITY.md:153 row.

T9.evidence: Repro: slices.yml with A and B both `paths: []`. Command (both languages): `<cli> slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --slices .goalloop-support/triage8/T9/slices.yml --attribute '<name:Leaf,to:A' --attribute '<name:T,to:B' --filter 'slice:B'` -> exit 0 both, byte-identical (cmp passed), summary.matching_time_ns 7, slices=[('A', 7)]. The same run's attribution engine resolves the sample to A (first matching descendant rule, '<name:Leaf,to:A', Leaf is on the stack) — the report itself shows A owning the 7 ns — yet the bottom filter slice:B accepted the sample. Code: Python _filter_matches (clankerprof/slices.py:~275-290) falls back to _sample_has_descendant_attribute_for_slice (slices.py:256-268), which returns true if ANY descendant rule targeting the named slice matches any stack frame, ignoring that an earlier rule wins; Rust sample_has_descendant_attribute_for_slice (slices.rs:~622-638, used by filter_matches_stack ~740) is the declared mirror. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:621-624 — 'Bottom slice:<name> filters evaluate the sample's **effective** attribution in both polarities: a sample rescued into a slice by a descendant attribute rule matches slice:<name>...'. The effective attribution is A (attribute rules apply in declaration order, first match wins, exactly as slice_for_sample implements), so slice:B must reject the sample. The output is even self-inconsistent: filtered-in time reported under a slice the filter named out. Identical in both languages — identical-but-harmful.
T9.subclaims: (1) Filter slice:B passes a sample whose winning rule targets A — TRUE, reproduced byte-identically in both languages. (2) The rescue is existence-based rather than precedence-based — TRUE by code inspection in both implementations (any-rule scan over options.attributes filtered to target_slice == name). (3) Spec requires effective attribution — TRUE, spec 621 says 'effective' explicitly and 'rescued INTO a slice' (the sample is rescued into A, not B). (4) Inverted polarity also affected — TRUE by the same code path (!slice:B would wrongly exclude), though the auditor only demonstrated the positive polarity.
T9.fix_sketch: Replace the existence scan with the winning-rule resolution. Python (clankerprof/slices.py _filter_matches, bottom slice branch): instead of `self._sample_has_descendant_attribute_for_slice(stack, item.value)`, rescue with `self._has_descendant_attributes and self.slice_for_sample(bottom, stack) == item.value` (slice_for_sample already implements declaration-order first-match over descendant+bottom rules, then path slices, then default — the documented effective attribution). Rust (slices.rs filter_matches_stack): replace sample_has_descendant_attribute_for_slice(...) with `slice_for_frame(bottom, stack, options, true) == value`. Delete or repurpose the old helpers. Decide-and-pin the residual question of whether the bottom frame's non-descendant slice should still match when a descendant rule reassigns the sample elsewhere (current first branch keeps it; the minimal fix leaves that branch untouched — if the spec's 'effective' wording is read strictly, tighten both branches together and say so in spec 621-624). Add parity + native tests: the repro (slice:B rejects, slice:A keeps), !slice:A / !slice:B polarity, and rule-order swap flipping the outcome. Update PARITY.md descendant-attribute row.

T10.evidence: Repro: `.venv/bin/python -m clankerprof.cli slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --output ''` -> exit 0, full JSON report (including the 7 ns (all) slice) on stdout — the explicit empty path is silently discarded, no receipt, no artifact. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --facts ... --output ''` -> exit 2, stderr envelope {"error": "error: a value is required for '--output <OUTPUT>' but none was supplied\n\nFor more information, try '--help'.", "ok": false} (clap's built-in PathBuf value parser rejects empty values; main.rs declares `output: Option<PathBuf>` at e.g. line 57). Python cause: truthiness guards `if args.output:` at clankerprof/cli.py:1171, 1196, 1292, 1322, 1359, 1377 (and `not args.output` in the compat-CSV check at 1138), so '' behaves as None. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:740-742 defines the with/without --output stream contract with no empty-value leniency anywhere (grep 'empty' shows none), and spec 776-779 requires usage errors ('invalid option values') to follow the exit-2 envelope through BOTH standalone CLIs. Same input, exit 0 + artifact-to-stdout vs exit 2 — a shared-CLI-surface parity defect, and the Python side is independently harmful (an explicitly supplied destination silently ignored; a script capturing stdout gets the report where it expected a receipt).
T10.subclaims: (1) Python treats explicit empty --output as absent across multiple command paths — TRUE (reproduced on slices; same truthiness guard verified by inspection at cli.py:1171/1196/1292/1322/1359/1377 for targets/slices/scopes/compare/facts, plus the 1138 compat-CSV interaction). (2) Rust rejects the value as a usage error — TRUE, exit 2 with the quoted clap envelope. (3) Divergence not documented as deliberate — TRUE: no spec text on empty option values; spec 776 folds invalid option values into the shared envelope contract for both standalone CLIs.
T10.fix_sketch: Fix Python to fail closed like Rust (empty path is never a writable destination — Path('').write_text raises FileNotFoundError anyway, which would leak an OS-error envelope at write time on the targets/compare paths). Add a small validator in clankerprof/cli.py applied at handler entry to every path-valued option (--output, --profile, --facts, --config, --slices, --runtime-rules, ...): empty string -> ValueError routed through the existing exit-2 envelope, mirroring clap's blanket empty-PathBuf rejection so the whole flag matrix converges, not just --output. Message: either hardcode clap's text for --output ("error: a value is required for '--output <OUTPUT>' but none was supplied\n\nFor more information, try '--help'.") for byte parity, or use a native message and pin the divergence — spec 776-782 already scopes usage-error prose as engine-specific, so a documented shared prefix + engine detail is acceptable; state the choice in CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md near line 776. Then normalize the guards (`if args.output is not None:` at cli.py:1138/1171/1196/1292/1322/1359/1377) so truthiness never reappears. Parity test: --output '' (and one other path flag with '') exits 2 in both languages.

T11.evidence: Repro (worktree root): `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --output .goalloop-support/triage8/T11/py_no.json` (exit 0) vs same + `--show-paths` -> py_yes.json (exit 0); `cmp -s py_no.json py_yes.json` reports IDENTICAL, and py_no.json already contains `"filename": "/srv/app/leaf.py"`. Same for Rust: `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json [--show-paths] --output ...` -> rs_no.json == rs_yes.json (IDENTICAL, both exit 0). Dead-flag confirmation: `grep -rn show_paths clankerprof/ crates/clankerprof-core/src/` shows the flag is parsed (clankerprof/cli.py:1219-1280, main.rs:816-878), stored on SliceAnalysisOptions (slices.py:62) / SliceOptions (slices.rs:46), but no renderer or analysis code reads it — render.py slice_payload (line ~972) and the Rust slice renderer emit `filename` unconditionally; slices has only a JSON renderer (render_slice_json, render.py:920). Yet the option is advertised as functional config in clankerprof/README.md:348 and docs/CLANKERPROF.md:449, exercised by the parity matrix (tests/test_clankerprof_rust_parity.py:1608 and the TOML config at :1402) which only proves the two no-ops match, and round-tripped by parity.py:378. docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md never mentions show_paths, and goalloop.audit.md has no refutation covering it — nothing documents the no-op as deliberate.
T11.subclaims: (1) Flag/config key accepted by both CLIs: TRUE. (2) Stored option never consulted; output byte-identical with and without: TRUE in both languages. (3) Filenames always emitted regardless: TRUE (render.py slice_payload includes `filename` unconditionally; Rust matches). (4) Parity test compares two equally inert implementations: TRUE — the matrix rows with/without --show-paths produce identical artifacts, so parity passes vacuously. (5) Advertised behavior: TRUE for README/CLANKERPROF.md (shown as a meaningful config option); the normative SPEC is silent, so this is a docs/CLI-surface honesty defect rather than a spec-text violation. Severity minor is fair.
T11.fix_sketch: Pick one semantics and land it in both languages plus docs together: (a) Implement the toggle — in clankerprof/render.py slice_payload emit the `filename` (and arguably `line`) key only when resolved_options.show_paths, mirror in crates/clankerprof-core/src/slices.rs's frame payload builder, regenerate parity fixtures, and document the field as conditional in docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md; or (b) if always-showing paths is the intended v2 contract (JSON consumers rely on `filename`), delete `--show-paths`/`show_paths` from cli.py, main.rs, SliceAnalysisOptions/SliceOptions, parity.py, the parity matrix, README.md and docs/CLANKERPROF.md. Either way add a test asserting the flag changes output (a) or is rejected (b), so the no-op cannot regress.

T12.evidence:
Repro: wrote .goalloop-support/triage8/T12/cfg.yml = `owenr:
  Intended: name:Leaf
scope:
  - function: T`. `uv run clankerprof scopes --facts .goalloop-support/triage4/K8/facts.json --config .goalloop-support/triage8/T12/cfg.yml --output py.json` -> exit 0, report contains `"domains": []`. Rust: `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- scopes --facts ... --config cfg.yml --output rs.json` -> exit 0, `"domains": []`, `cmp` IDENTICAL. Code: clankerprof/cli.py `_load_boundary_options` (~line 1038) reads only `slices` plus the aliased `cost_kind|category`, `owner|domain`, `scope|boundary` sections via payload.get/_aliased_config_value and never checks for unrecognized top-level keys; crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs config load mirrors this. Spec contract: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:419-423 — 'Scope/boundary entries are fixed-shape: an unknown entry key is a validation error naming the key (`Unknown scope field: <key>.`) ... a typo such as `exclude_descendents` must never silently disable its rule, mirroring the rule-pack unknown-key principle', and line 287 mandates unknown-top-level-key rejection for rule packs. The R7-06 fix (goalloop.audit.md:97) established exactly this principle for entry keys; the top level of the same config file violates it: a typo'd `owner`/`cost_kind` section silently removes all attribution/exclusions. No spec text or audit refutation documents open top-level maps as deliberate.
T12.subclaims: (1) Unknown-key validation exists for scope entries but not the config top level: TRUE (entry check landed in R7-06; _load_boundary_options has no top-level allowlist). (2) Both engines exit 0 and drop the misspelled section: TRUE, byte-identical reports with `"domains": []`. (3) Silent removal of attribution/exclusions: TRUE — a misspelled `owner:` yields empty domains and a misspelled top-level section holding exclusions would likewise vanish. (4) Contract violation: TRUE in spirit and by the spec's own stated principle (never silently disable a rule via a typo; rule packs already reject unknown top-level keys); the spec does not carve out top-level scope-config keys as intentionally open. Major severity is justified.
T12.fix_sketch: Python: in `_load_boundary_options` (clankerprof/cli.py), after `_load_config_payload`, reject any key outside {slices, cost_kind, category, owner, domain, scope, boundary} with a shared message such as `Unknown scope config field: <key>.` — report the first offending key in deterministic (sorted) order for byte parity. Rust: identical allowlist + message in the scopes config loader in crates/clankerprof-core/src/scopes.rs. Update docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:419ff to state top-level scope configs are fixed-shape too, and add Python+Rust tests plus a parity error-case pinning the message. Audit the slices YAML config loader (cli.py lines ~175-223) for the same gap as follow-up, since it reads keys equally loosely.

T13.evidence: Repro: `printf '\x32\x00\x32\x03cpu\x32\x0bnanoseconds\x5a\x02\x08\x01\x5a\x02\x10\x02' > prof.pb` (string table ["","cpu","nanoseconds"]; two field-11 period_type submessages: {type_index:1} then {unit_index:2}). `uv run clankerprof facts --profile prof.pb --output py.json` -> exit 0, `"period_type":{"type":"","unit":"nanoseconds"}`. `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- facts --profile prof.pb --output rs.json` -> exit 0, same bytes, cmp IDENTICAL. Code: clankerprof/proto.py decode_profile_bytes, field 11 branch does `raw_period_type = _parse_value_type(...)` — full replacement; crates/clankerprof-core/src/proto.rs:~207-222 does the same. Protobuf encoding spec ('Last One Wins' / message merging): for a non-repeated embedded-message field, parsers must merge multiple instances as if by MergeFrom — so a conformant decoder (e.g. Go's, the pprof reference) yields {type:"cpu",unit:"nanoseconds"}. docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:29-36 requires the decoder to preserve 'the profile's declared ... period_type' and lines 42-48 make it strict about malformed input; this input is valid protobuf and its declared metadata is lost. No spec text documents replace-over-merge as deliberate (grep for merge/last-wins in the spec finds only YAML/JSON duplicate-key rules), and goalloop.audit.md has no refutation on it.
T13.subclaims: (1) Both decoders overwrite the previous period_type on repeated field 11: TRUE (proto.py assignment; proto.rs equivalent). (2) The handcrafted bytes are a valid split-field encoding whose reference-decoded value is {cpu, nanoseconds}: TRUE per protobuf singular-message merge semantics. (3) Both engines emit {"","nanoseconds"}: TRUE, identical in both — parity holds but both are wrong versus the reference protobuf semantics the spec's own signed-int64 rules already defer to. Genuine identical-but-harmful correctness defect; impact limited to profile metadata (period_type feeds facts export, not primary-value math), so minor is right.
T13.fix_sketch: Accumulate rather than replace, using byte concatenation (which is exactly protobuf message merging for scalar-field submessages): Python — in decode_profile_bytes keep `period_type_bytes = bytearray()`; on each field-11 occurrence `period_type_bytes += reader.read_length_delimited()`; after the loop, `raw_period_type = _parse_value_type(bytes(period_type_bytes)) if period_type_bytes_seen else None` (track a seen flag so `\x5a\x00` still yields an empty-but-present period_type). Rust — same pattern in proto.rs: extend a Vec<u8> per occurrence, parse once after the scan. Single-occurrence profiles are byte-for-byte unchanged. Add the T13 bytes as a shared decode test in tests/ and crates/clankerprof-core, pinning {"type":"cpu","unit":"nanoseconds"}, and note the merge rule next to the signed-int64 sentence in docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:38-41.

T14.evidence: Repro: from .goalloop-support/triage4/K14/facts_valid.json built facts_bad.json with value_types=[{count,count},{cpu,nanoseconds}], default_sample_type="count", primary_value_index=1, sample values [1,100], summary total_primary_value=100. `uv run clankerprof slices --facts .goalloop-support/triage8/T14/facts_bad.json --output py.json` -> exit 0, rows report `"time_ns": 100`; `cargo run -q -p clankerprof-core --bin clankerprof-rs -- slices --facts facts_bad.json --output rs.json` -> exit 0, identical bytes (cmp IDENTICAL). Code: clankerprof/facts.py _validated_profile_meta (lines ~368-393) and crates/clankerprof-core/src/facts.rs sample_facts_from_v2 (lines ~250-280) check presence, integer type, non-negativity, and the R7-02 range bound only — no consistency check against default_sample_type. Spec: docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:52-58 defines the primary index as a deterministic function of default_sample_type and value_types (rule 1: a named declared type's position — here `count` at index 0); line 91 defines profile.primary_value_index as 'Selected primary value index (see primary-value selection)'; lines 147-150 justify the range rejection with '(no selection rule can produce one)' — the identical rationale applies to this artifact, which no selection rule can produce either; lines 97-101 establish the precedent that redundant fields (summary) are cross-validated and 'mismatches are validation errors'. goalloop.audit.md R5-08 (#69) and R7-02 (#93) show the deliberate direction is strict v2 metadata validation rejecting self-consistent-but-wrong artifacts; nothing documents index-authoritative-over-selection as intended.
T14.subclaims: (1) R7 fix bounds the index but does not tie it to the declared selection: TRUE in both importers. (2) Contradictory artifact accepted and analyzed with index 1 (time_ns 100) instead of the contract-selected count metric at index 0: TRUE, byte-identical in both engines. (3) 'Should be rejected' per contract: TRUE by the spec's own definition of the field and its 'no selection rule can produce one' rejection rationale, plus the established redundancy-cross-check precedent for summary. Major is fair: a tampered/buggy artifact silently reports the wrong metric with a self-consistent summary, exactly the failure class R7-02 fixed at the boundary but not in the interior.
T14.fix_sketch: In v2 import only (v1 keeps documented leniency): recompute the expected index with the shared selection routine — if default_sample_type is nonempty and names a declared type, its (first) position; else last declared index; else 0 — and reject on mismatch with a shared message, e.g. `Sample facts primary_value_index <n> contradicts the declared default sample type selection (expected <m>).`. Python: extend _validated_profile_meta in clankerprof/facts.py after the range check, reusing the same helper the profile decoder uses so duplicate type names resolve identically. Rust: same check after the index_in_range block in sample_facts_from_v2 (crates/clankerprof-core/src/facts.rs), byte-identical message. All exporter-produced artifacts pass by construction. Update docs/CLANKERPROF_SPEC.md:147-152 to name the consistency rejection, add Python+Rust unit tests plus a parity error-case, and cover with the appropriate compliance-matrix ID if one governs facts import strictness.
